content.text: Every program is a sequence of instructions, but real-world logic is never purely linear. Control flow is the mechanism by which a program decides
to skip or repeat them. Mastering control flow is what separates code that merely runs from code that reasons.

Control flow refers to the order in which individual statements, instructions, and function calls are executed at runtime. By default, execution proceeds top-to-bottom, one line at a time. Control flow constructs interrupt this default sequence to express conditional logic, repetition, and early exits.
The three fundamental control flow patterns are:
— execute different code based on a condition (
These three patterns, and their combinations, are sufficient to express any computable algorithm — a fact rooted in structured programming theory.
A conditional block tests a boolean expression and executes its body only if the expression evaluates to
clause is the fallback when no condition holds.
in order from top to bottom
, and only the first matching branch executes. Once a branch is taken, all subsequent
clauses are skipped entirely — even if a later condition would also be true.
Any expression can serve as a condition. The runtime coerces the result to boolean using each language's truthiness rules.
— code that drifts rightward and becomes hard to follow. The idiomatic solution is
using guard clauses: validate preconditions at the top of a function and return immediately on failure, keeping the happy path at the lowest indentation level.
The guard clause version reads as a checklist of requirements, and the core logic (
) stands clearly at the end without indentation noise.
When branching on the value of a single expression with many possible cases,

command = "quit"

match command:
    case "quit":
        quit_program()
    case "help":
        show_help()
    case "start" | "begin":     # OR pattern
        start_program()
    case _:                      # wildcard / default
        print(f"Unknown command: {command}")
goes far beyond value matching — it supports destructuring, guards, and type patterns:

def handle_response(response):
    match response:
        case {"status": 200, "data": data}:
            process(data)
        case {"status": 404}:
            raise NotFoundError()
        case {"status": int(code)} if code >= 500:
            raise ServerError(code)
        case _:
            raise UnexpectedResponse(response)
statements, execution continues into the next case unless an explicit
terminates it. This is a notorious source of bugs. Forgetting a
is one of the most common mistakes in C, Java, and JavaScript. Many style guides mandate always including
— a sequence, collection, generator, or any object implementing the iterator protocol. It does not use an index counter by default:
When iteration over numbers is needed,
generates an arithmetic sequence on demand without creating a list in memory:
— it produces each value on demand and uses O(1) memory regardless of the range size.
A common beginner mistake is using
to get both an index and a value. The idiomatic approach is
To iterate over multiple sequences in lockstep:

names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
scores = [92, 87, 95]

for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f"{name}: {score}")

# zip stops at the shortest iterable by default
# Use itertools.zip_longest to continue to the end of the longest
from itertools import zip_longest
for a, b in zip_longest([1, 2, 3], [10, 20], fillvalue=0):
    print(a, b)   # 1 10 / 2 20 / 3 0
Languages in the C family use a three-part
header: initialization, condition, and increment. This is more explicit but also more error-prone:
loop repeats its body as long as its condition evaluates to
. It is the correct tool when the number of iterations is not known in advance.

# Basic while loop
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Reading until a sentinel value
user_input = ""
while user_input != "quit":
    user_input = input("Enter command: ")
    process(user_input)

# Infinite loop with explicit break
while True:
    packet = receive_packet()
    if packet is None:
        break
    handle(packet)
, checking the condition after the first iteration. Python does not have a
construct; the pattern is simulated with
when iterating over a known sequence or collection, or when the number of iterations is determined before the loop begins.
when the termination condition depends on state that changes inside the loop — network responses, user input, convergence criteria, file parsing.
terminates the innermost enclosing loop and transfers execution to the first statement after the loop:
when continuing to iterate would waste work:
skips the remainder of the current loop body and jumps to the next iteration's condition check:

# Process only valid lines, skip errors
for line in log_file:
    if line.startswith("#"):    # comment line
        continue
    if not line.strip():        # blank line
        continue
    parse_log_entry(line)

# Equivalent without continue (more deeply nested)
for line in log_file:
    if not line.startswith("#") and line.strip():
        parse_log_entry(line)
is especially valuable when filtering has multiple independent conditions. The flat structure with
is a statement that does nothing. It exists because Python's syntax requires at least one statement in every block. It is used as a placeholder during development:
clause on loops that executes only if the loop completed
. This is ideal for search patterns where you need to know whether the loop terminated early or ran to completion:
, this pattern requires a flag variable:
version is cleaner and eliminates the flag, but the feature is widely misunderstood. The name is misleading — it does not mean "else run this if the iterable is empty." The mental model is: the
runs if and only if the loop was not exited by
Loops can be nested to arbitrary depth. Each level of nesting multiplies complexity. A double loop is O(n²), a triple loop O(n³), and so on.
only exits the innermost loop. To break out of multiple levels, use one of these approaches:
Refactoring the loop into a function (approach 2) is almost always the cleanest solution because
Python's comprehension syntax offers a concise, readable, and typically faster alternative to building collections with explicit loops.

# Imperative
squares = []
for x in range(10):
    if x % 2 == 0:
        squares.append(x ** 2)

# Comprehension
squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
# [0, 4, 16, 36, 64]
A generator expression is a lazy comprehension — it produces values on demand without building the entire collection in memory. Use it when you only need to iterate over the result once:

# Loads all squares into memory
squares_list = [x ** 2 for x in range(1_000_000)]

# Produces squares lazily — O(1) memory
squares_gen = (x ** 2 for x in range(1_000_000))

# Particularly powerful with aggregation functions
total = sum(x ** 2 for x in range(1_000_000))
maximum = max(len(line) for line in open("file.txt"))
Comprehensions can nest to flatten nested structures, though readability degrades quickly beyond two levels:

# Flatten a 2D matrix
matrix = [[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]
flat = [val for row in matrix for val in row]
# [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9]

# The order of for clauses mirrors the imperative version:
# for row in matrix:
#     for val in row:
#         flat.append(val)

# Running total, max, or aggregation
total = 0
for transaction in transactions:
    total += transaction.amount

# More Pythonic with built-ins
total = sum(t.amount for t in transactions)
largest = max(t.amount for t in transactions)
. Both accept generators, making them efficient alternatives to explicit loops:

# Check if any item passes a condition
has_error = any(item.status == "error" for item in results)

# Check if all items pass a condition
all_valid = all(item.is_valid() for item in records)

# Equivalent loop (verbose)
has_error = False
for item in results:
    if item.status == "error":
        has_error = True
        break
The following ties together conditionals, loops,
, and comprehensions in a realistic context — parsing structured log files to extract and aggregate error events.
used for multiple independent filter conditions, keeping the logic flat
1. Modifying a collection while iterating over it
Mutating a list, dict, or set during iteration leads to skipped elements or runtime errors:

# Bug: items are skipped because list shrinks mid-iteration
items = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
for item in items:
    if item % 2 == 0:
        items.remove(item)    # skips elements!

# Correct: iterate over a copy, or build a new list
items = [item for item in items if item % 2 != 0]

# For dicts in Python 3: RuntimeError if size changes during iteration
for key in dict.keys():          # Error if keys are added/removed
    del dict[key]

for key in list(dict.keys()):    # Safe: iterate over a snapshot
    del dict[key]

# Print elements at indices 0 through 4
n = 5
for i in range(n):       # correct: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
    print(data[i])

for i in range(1, n):    # wrong: skips index 0
    print(data[i])

for i in range(n + 1):   # wrong: IndexError on data[5]
    print(data[i])
3. Infinite loops from missing state update

# Bug: count never changes — infinite loop
count = 0
while count < 10:
    print("hello")
    # forgot: count += 1

# Common misreading: else runs when list is empty
# Actual behavior: else runs when no break occurred

for item in []:         # empty list
    process(item)
else:
    print("This DOES run — no break occurred")
    # Often confused with "ran zero iterations"
5. Using a loop when a built-in exists

# Slow and verbose
total = 0
for x in numbers:
    total += x

# Correct
total = sum(numbers)

# Checking containment
found = False
for x in numbers:
    if x == target:
        found = True
        break

# Correct
found = target in numbers        # O(n) for list
found = target in number_set     # O(1) for set
) is cleaner, less error-prone, and more idiomatic in Python than indexing with
. Reserve index access for cases where the index itself is needed.
When both index and value are needed,
is idiomatic and eliminates off-by-one errors.
If a loop body grows beyond 10–15 lines, extract it into a named function. Short loop bodies are easier to test, read, and modify.
Comprehensions are for building values, not for executing side effects. If the goal is to call a function for its effect rather than collect its result, use an explicit

# Wrong: comprehension for side effects
[print(x) for x in items]

# Right: loop for side effects
for x in items:
    print(x)
More than two levels of nested loops is a signal to refactor. Extract inner loops into functions, use itertools for complex iteration patterns, or restructure the data to reduce nesting.
module provides efficient, composable iteration tools:

Python loops have significant per-iteration overhead compared to compiled languages because each iteration involves bytecode interpretation, attribute lookups, and dynamic dispatch. For hot loops over large datasets, prefer:
loops because they run in optimized C internally.
— replace Python loops entirely for numerical arrays. A NumPy operation on a million elements runs in C with SIMD instructions, often 100–1000x faster.

import numpy as np

# Python loop: slow for large arrays
result = []
for x in data:
    result.append(x * 2 + 1)

# NumPy vectorized: fast
arr = np.array(data)
result = arr * 2 + 1   # operates on entire array at C speed
For processing large files or streams, generator pipelines allow data to flow through transformation stages one element at a time, using O(1) memory regardless of input size:
This pattern processes files of arbitrary size with constant memory consumption.
Algorithmic Complexity Matters More Than Micro-Optimization
The biggest performance gains come from choosing better algorithms, not tuning loop syntax. A nested O(n²) loop replaced with a hash-based O(n) lookup dwarfs any micro-optimization:

# O(n²): for each item in A, scan all of B
common = [x for x in list_a if x in list_b]

# O(n): convert B to a set for O(1) lookups
set_b = set(list_b)
common = [x for x in list_a if x in set_b]
Think of a program's control flow as a
. The main sequence is the river's main channel. Conditional branches (
— the water chooses one path based on conditions. Loops are
— circular currents that keep water cycling until some force (
, a failed condition) releases it back into the main flow.
that bypasses a section of the eddy for one cycle.
— an abrupt exit to a lower level.
The goal of good control flow design is a clean, readable river — not a tangled delta of nested branches and unmarked tributaries.
) are the three categories of control flow that together express all possible algorithmic logic.
Conditions are evaluated top-to-bottom and short-circuit; only the first matching branch executes in a chain of
Guard clauses with early returns flatten deeply nested conditionals into readable checklists of preconditions.
loops iterate over sequences and known collections;
loops iterate when the termination condition depends on runtime state.
skips to the next iteration; Python's
Modifying a collection during iteration is a common source of bugs; always iterate over a copy or build a new collection.
Generator expressions and pipelines process arbitrarily large data in O(1) memory, making them the correct tool for streaming and file-based workloads.
Algorithmic improvements — like replacing O(n²) nested loops with O(n) hash lookups — yield far larger gains than any loop-level micro-optimization.
